coverage_map.coverage_assessment: The dataset heavily emphasizes basic set operations (union, intersection, difference, complement) evaluated either through direct numerical definitions or via abstract logical translation. There is a total absence of 3-set Venn diagram word problems, algebraic applications of De Morgan's laws, and cross-topic integrations. The focus is entirely on definition precision and interpreting English qualifiers ('or but not both') into strict set notation.

easy.steps_required: 1 to 2 steps (direct application of a provided definition or formula)
easy.answer_similarity: Low; numerical gaps or conceptually distinct logical statements

medium.steps_required: 3 to 5 steps (evaluate multiple sub-conditions, then aggregate)
medium.concept_mixing: Basic arithmetic (divisibility rules) combined with set boundaries
medium.answer_similarity: High; options match outputs of missed logical steps

hard.answer_similarity: Highly engineered distractors reflecting single-element omissions

distractor_engine.input_structure: Sets A, B; exclusionary qualifier ('or but not both', 'nonempty')
distractor_engine.correct_transformation: Apply boundary exclusion: |A|+|B|-2|A∩B| (Symmetric Difference) or 2^n - 1 (Nonempty Subsets)
finite_distractor_set: |A|+|B| — Naive sum ignoring overlap entirely, maps to M1Q22: 53
2^n — Raw power set missing empty set subtraction, maps to M1Q30: 16
True statements masquerading as correct when prompt asks 'which is false' (M2Q15: A, C, D, E)

traps.name: Symmetric Difference Omission
traps.mechanism: Student applies pure union formula |A|+|B|-|A∩B| instead of symmetric difference |A|+|B|-2|A∩B| because they miss the 'but not both' qualifier

traps.name: Empty Set Subtraction Omission
traps.mechanism: Student computes 2^n total subsets but forgets to subtract the empty set when the question asks for nonempty subsets

traps.name: False Qualifier Inversion
traps.mechanism: Question asks which statement is false. Students under time pressure select the first statement they confirm as true rather than systematically verifying all options to find the false one

M1Q22.A: Pure union with intersection subtracted once: (33-6)+(20-6)+6 = 47
M1Q22.B: Naive sum with no overlap adjustment: 33+20 = 53
M1Q22.E: Multiples of 15 only (intersection): 6 — or multiples of 5 only: 20

M1Q30.A: Confused subsets with elements — returned n=4
M1Q30.D: Used n! = 4! = 24 — permutations instead of subsets
M1Q30.E: Correct 2^n=16 but forgot to subtract empty set

M3Q39.A: Overgeneralization — empty intersection does not require A to be empty
M3Q39.C: Irrelevant cardinality claim — disjoint sets can have equal size
M3Q39.D: Overgeneralization — A = B^c only if A and B partition the universal set
M3Q39.E: Overgeneralization — empty intersection does not require B to be empty

M4Q8.A: Confused difference with complement — assumed no overlap means empty result
M4Q8.B: Returned B-only elements ({5}) instead of A-only elements
M4Q8.D: False constraint — the definition works even when B has elements not in A
M4Q8.E: Included B-only elements in the result — confused A\B with A△B

time_pressure_anatomy.description: Time pressure manifests through translation friction. Converting 'divisible by 3 or by 5 but not by both' into the symmetric difference operation requires deliberate cognitive pausing. M2Q15 forces sequential evaluation of 5 distinct set computations just to find the one false statement, inducing a rush to select the first plausible set.

calibrated_uplift.key_principle: Bocconi difficulty = familiar problem + evil answer choices + slight ambiguity + time pressure.
calibrated_uplift.layer_1_problem_structure_mutation: Taking standard operations (Union) and adding exclusionary boundaries (A \ B or symmetric difference).
calibrated_uplift.layer_2_cognitive_load_injection: Phrasing questions in the negative ('which of the following statements is false').
calibrated_uplift.layer_3_answer_engineering: MOST WEIGHT. Every intermediate logical error serves as a distractor. If a formula requires a subtraction step, the unadjusted raw output will exactly match a trap option.

figure_passage_interaction_patterns.note: No figures. The passage equivalent is mathematical notation (A \ B, A^c). Risk is entirely embedded in notation-to-prose translation. When the prompt provides a definition, the trap centers on boundary edge cases.

ambiguity_patterns.primary_ambiguity_mechanism: Qualified language restricts a standard mathematical concept. 'Subsets' becomes 'nonempty subsets.' The standard mathematical 'or' is inclusive; the prompt forces exclusive OR, testing if the student reads the entire sentence.
ambiguity_patterns.near_correct_construction: Second-closest options match the output of applying the correct formula but with one boundary condition omitted (e.g., pure union instead of symmetric difference, or 2^n instead of 2^n-1).

generation_fingerprint:
## SETS GENERATION FINGERPRINT

Bocconi Sets evaluates fundamental set definitions against exclusionary language. Difficulty is not driven by Sets theory complexity — it is driven entirely by answer choice engineering around finite boundary omissions. The arithmetic is trivially simple; the challenge is precision in identifying which boundary condition the student is expected to apply.

Dominant question types: element counting via inclusion-exclusion (symmetric difference, union, intersection), evaluating logical equivalence of set operations, and counting subsets with constraints (nonempty, proper subsets).

DISTRACTOR ENGINE: Every distractor is the exact numerical output of a specific omission error.
- Symmetric Difference Omission: student applies pure union |A∪B| = |A|+|B|-|A∩B| instead of |A|+|B|-2|A∩B|. Must appear as a distractor whenever symmetric difference is the correct operation.
- Empty Set Omission: student computes 2^n instead of 2^n - 1 when counting nonempty subsets.
- Over-subtraction: student subtracts the intersection three times instead of twice.
- True-for-False Swap: when the question asks which statement is false, exactly 4 options are true. Time pressure causes students to select the first true option they verify.

ANSWER ENGINEERING MODEL: 1 correct (applies all boundary conditions) + 1 symmetric difference omission + 1 empty set omission + 1 over-subtraction + 1 scope shift (applies rule to wrong set).

QUALIFIER INVENTORY: nonempty, proper subset, difference, or but not both, false, complement. These are precision triggers — each one changes the formula applied.

CALIBRATED UPLIFT: Easy = direct numerical set given. Medium = abstract variable sets requiring logical translation of English qualifier. Hard = nested conditions where applying one rule changes the domain for the next.

Adhere to the Elimination Mapping Rule: every wrong option must correspond to a specific traceable student error — the generator must be able to state exactly which mistake leads to selecting each distractor.
